topics.answer: In the cited laboratory assay, female Drosophila suzukii preferred the harder oviposition texture. Drugs targeting TRP and DEG/ENaC channel families abolished that preference. This separates the observed behavior from the candidate mechanism: mechanosensitive channels are implicated, but the experiment did not identify one uniquely causal channel. It also does not show that fruit hardness alone determines field infestation or that one channel is a proven commercial target.

In the cited artificial-substrate assay, Drosophila suzukii laid fewer eggs on soft 1% agar with visible commensal microbial growth than on the matched uninoculated substrate. By contrast, Drosophila melanogaster and Drosophila biarmipes preferred the microbe-rich soft substrate and laid more eggs there. On hard 3% agar, D. suzukii showed no significant preference or aversion to microbial growth. The hardness-dependent change implicates an interaction between mechanosensory and chemosensory information; it does not identify the responsible microbial molecule or show that every fruit microbe deters SWD in crops. To isolate the interaction, cross microbial treatment with matched agar hardness instead of changing microbes and texture together.

Coconut free fatty acid (CFFA) deterred SWD oviposition dose-dependently in laboratory two-choice tests, with a 99% reduction at 20 mg versus solvent control, and field raspberries treated at 20 mg received 64% fewer eggs. Caprylic and capric acids were active components; at the ratio and concentrations present in CFFA, their two-component blend was about as effective as the full mixture, while either acid alone was less effective. These egg-count assays do not isolate spatial avoidance from contact, substrate chemistry, or post-contact oviposition deterrence. Resolve mode with a no-contact assay using measured airborne concentration, followed separately by treated-substrate choice, total egg output, activity, survival, and field fruit endpoints.

The cited comparison separates larval nutrition from adult oviposition. Drosophila suzukii larvae performed better at lower protein concentrations and consumed less protein-rich diets than Drosophila biarmipes. Adults differed in oviposition preference for substrate hardness, but not nutritional composition. The study therefore supports broader use of ripening and softer food niches, not an adult protein-to-carbohydrate oviposition mechanism. In product assays, manipulate texture and nutrition independently and keep larval performance, larval intake, adult egg allocation, and total fecundity as separate endpoints.

Both adult density and host quality changed Drosophila suzukii oviposition in the cited laboratory experiments. Whole raspberries supported substantially more oviposition than lower-quality raspberry agar. On whole raspberries, eggs per female declined as female density increased; on raspberry agar, intermediate densities produced the highest per-female oviposition. Male density did not significantly change oviposition on whole raspberries, whereas the response to male density on agar was nonlinear. Total eggs, eggs per female, and egg distribution among hosts are different outcomes and should not be collapsed. These laboratory density and substrate effects do not directly predict field infestation.

The study tested 2-phenylethanol at 0.01-0.1%, ethanol at 1-10%, and acetic acid at 0.1-1%. In Y-tube choices, Drosophila melanogaster favored the higher tested concentrations more strongly, whereas Drosophila suzukii tended toward lower concentrations. Electroantennogram responses measured antennal detection separately from choice. Mated females favored higher concentrations more than virgins only in the D. melanogaster comparison, so that mating-state result cannot be assigned to SWD. Keep chemical identity, concentration, Y-tube behavior, and electroantennogram response separate; the study does not show that all fermentation volatiles repel SWD.

Methyl jasmonate was not uniformly repellent. In the cited planar olfactometer, 309.0 micrograms per filter paper was repellent, while 3.86 to 15.45 micrograms per filter paper was attractive. In a separate two-choice cage, doses above 1287.5 micrograms per filter paper reduced oviposition by more than 90 percent on artificial fruits. Applied mass per filter paper is not measured air concentration or a crop application rate, and neither endpoint by itself proves field crop protection.

Fewer eggs on a treated choice cannot by itself establish sensory repellency. Interpret egg allocation alongside total eggs or a no-choice fecundity measure, survival, locomotion or dwell behavior, and standardized mating and physiological state. The cited pre-exposure study separately measured trap capture, female survival, and oviposition frequency, illustrating why one egg endpoint is insufficient. Choice, activity, toxicity, mating, and fecundity must remain separate explanations.

Measure orientation and egg laying as separate endpoints on separate timelines. In a non-contact airflow arena, prevent surface contact and record approach, heading, entry, zone occupancy, exit, dwell time, speed, distance, and immobility at fixed intervals. In parallel contact-permitted choice and no-choice oviposition assays, count both egg allocation and total eggs over time, then move flies to clean untreated substrate and measure post-exposure catch-up. An immediate directional shift away from the odor with normal locomotion before egg laying supports spatial avoidance. Normal approach and occupancy followed by later egg deposition, reduced total egg output, or catch-up after odor removal supports delayed or suppressed oviposition instead. Use carrier and airflow shams, reverse treatment side, randomize order, and standardize age, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate, and circadian time. Track knockdown, survival, and recovery separately so motor impairment is not mislabeled as repellency. This measurement chain distinguishes the endpoints; it does not by itself prove field crop protection.

Use both a matched untreated control and a carrier-only or solvent-only control. Match airflow direction and speed, ventilation, source position, and release rate between sides; run a clean-air sham, randomize or reverse treatment side and order, and use independent biological replicates. To distinguish directed odor avoidance from impaired movement, separately measure heading or zone choice alongside flight or walking speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff, knockdown, survival, and recovery in matched clean air. Preserve total eggs or a matched no-choice fecundity endpoint so reallocation between sides is not mistaken for reduced oviposition. Standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, prior egg laying, substrate condition, and circadian time. Selective orientation away from the odor with normal clean-air locomotion supports repellency; slowing or immobility in both settings supports impairment instead. A two-choice laboratory result still does not prove field crop protection.

A Y-tube or planar olfactometer measures orientation or choice under that apparatus. It does not by itself measure egg laying, larval survival, fruit damage, or marketable yield. In the methyl jasmonate study, oviposition deterrence was measured separately in a two-choice cage with artificial fruits. Name the actual endpoint before making a product claim and require direct crop and damage outcomes before concluding crop protection.

Match fruit species, cultivar, ripeness, firmness, injury, and surface moisture between treatment and control, or vary them deliberately in a factorial design. Record those variables and randomize or block them across positions. The cited blueberry experiment showed that fruit injury directly changed oviposition and interacted hierarchically with other cues. It did not measure a specific moisture effect, so moisture control is an experimental-design recommendation rather than that paper's result.

Follow eggs with larval or pupal emergence, fruit infestation or damage, and marketable yield or loss in replicated field conditions. The cited push-pull study found fewer pupae emerging from treated raspberries, which is stronger than an egg-only endpoint. It still does not provide the complete package of crop damage, marketable yield, persistence, and operational fit needed for a commercial crop-protection claim.

Untreated Aedes aegypti host seeking combines carbon dioxide, skin odor, visual contrast, thermal cues, and other close-range information rather than one signal. Genetic removal of two visual rhodopsins abolished vision-guided target attraction while odor tracking remained normal, showing separable pathways. The arm-in-cage study also shows that contact and bite endpoints can diverge. None of this proves which channels remain intact after a particular repellent: compound-specific exposure must be tested while keeping activation, orientation, landing, contact, probing, and biting distinct.

Two 1979 primary studies support distinct post-blood-meal processes. The abdominal-distention study found that a replete blood meal and saline enemas immediately inhibited host seeking. The oocyte-maturation study separated egg development from feeding by using blood enemas; females that developed eggs were inhibited during oocyte maturation, and surgical manipulation plus haemolymph transfer implicated a circulating inhibitory factor. A 2013 genetic study describes roughly three days of suppression, treats Head Peptide-I as a candidate signal, and shows that NPYLR1 is not required. Standardizing feeding status, time since feeding, abdominal distention, egg-development state, and mating or insemination state is an R&D recommendation, not a universal protocol established by these studies. The evidence does not establish one receptor, the anatomical location of a distention sensor, or a universal 24-hour phase across the gonotrophic cycle.

No. NPYLR1 responded to Head Peptide-I, but NPYLR1 null mutants still showed normal post-blood-meal host-seeking suppression. In that study NPYLR1 was therefore not required in vivo. The result does not distinguish redundant signaling through other receptors from signaling through a different unknown receptor. Ligand or receptor activation is not the same as genetic necessity, and this result should not be confused with later work on NPYLR7 agonists.

Measure detection and avoidance independently at the same delivered dose. First test peripheral or central sensory responses with a solvent blank, a known-odor positive control, and a candidate dose-response; an electroantennogram is a broad antennal measure, so single-sensillum or calcium recordings may be needed to localize a response. Separately, use a non-contact choice, flight, or landing assay and record approach, occupancy, exit, and landing while also measuring locomotion, knockdown, survival, and recovery. An intact neural response without avoidance shows detection without demonstrated aversive behavior; it can also reflect a response below the behavioral threshold or the wrong context. No measured neural response plus no avoidance is only consistent with failure of the measured pathway if the positive controls and movement remain intact; it is not proof that every sensory route failed. For causality, disrupt the candidate receptor or pathway in a matched genetic background and rescue it, then repeat both neural and behavioral tests. The cited Aedes aegypti study illustrates the logic: pure transfluthrin repelled females in a non-contact hand-in-cage assay at 1 ppm without an electroantennogram signal, and repellency persisted in orco mutants but fell in sodium-channel kdr mutants. DEET showed a different Orco-dependent pattern. Thus a behavioral effect, antennal signal, receptor response, or mutation alone cannot establish the whole detection-to-avoidance mechanism.

A 150-person cohort showed differential Aedes aegypti attraction and preliminary models in which measured blood metabolites contributed to explained variation. Histidine-pathway metabolites were negatively correlated with attraction, while long-chain fatty acids and monoacylglycerols were positively correlated. These are associations, not proof that changing a metabolite changes attraction. For repellent assays, treat donor identity and sampling day as design variables: repeat collections, balance donor order across treatments, include multiple donors, and use donor as a blocking or repeated-measures factor. Keep blood metabolites separate from skin emissions, receptor responses, odor-sample choice, landing, and biting. A separate human-odor study found stable attraction differences across repeated samples from the same people over months to years; that supports repeated donor sampling but still does not make the metabolite correlations causal.

If mosquitoes briefly touch treated skin before bites decline, the result does not prove repellency at a distance because physical contact already occurred. In the cited tracking study, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts; that separates contact from biting but does not identify whether the post-contact effect was irritancy, taste-mediated deterrence, or another mechanism. Operationally, spatial repellency is movement away or reduced entry without physical contact, while contact irritancy is locomotor excitation or escape after touching treatment. A paired excito-repellency design separates non-contact and contact exposure and should also record knockdown and mortality. To establish a distance effect, prevent contact with a barrier and measure entry, orientation, landing, or escape before any touch, alongside a matched contact arm. Escape from a contact chamber does not prove spatial repellency, and reduced bites or landings alone do not establish an olfactory mechanism.

Use paired non-contact treatment and carrier-only control chambers with a mesh barrier that prevents surface contact. Record escape at 1-minute intervals for 30 minutes and compare escape-time curves; that is the direct behavioral measure of spatial avoidance in the cited assay. Run a matched contact format to separate contact excitation from the non-contact response. At 30 minutes, record knockdown separately among escaped and remaining mosquitoes, then follow recovery and 24-hour mortality. In this assay, greater non-contact escape indicates stronger spatial avoidance only when locomotor capacity is preserved. Reduced escape can indicate knockdown or impaired movement and must not be labeled repellency; in a differently designed assay, reduced entry or occupancy can indicate avoidance only when the endpoint direction and motor controls are explicit. Knockdown can suppress escape and mimic a weak behavioral response. Keep spatial avoidance, contact excitation, reversible knockdown, and death as separate endpoints.

The cited transfluthrin study directly supports matched treated and untreated chambers, recording temperature and relative humidity, separating contact from non-contact exposure, and keeping behavior, knockdown, and mortality distinct. It reports initial transfluthrin loading on the papers but says the active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Separately, the methods do not report a chamber-air concentration or plume measurement; that is an exposure-measurement gap, not the paper's named treated-paper residue limitation. The paper does not provide a universal standard for airflow direction and speed, plume sampling, side reversal, or every occupied-room and outdoor setting. For a new product assay, define and monitor airflow direction and speed, sample the plume or air concentration where possible, randomize or reverse treatment side, and keep carrier, release rate, geometry, and measured exposure explicit. Those are R&D design recommendations, not requirements established by this one paper; keep still-air conclusions separate from directional-flow conclusions. The cited public evidence does not provide one complete product-specific carrier, release-rate, application-method, and delivery package, so that remains a source gap rather than a basis for filling those details by inference.

No. Chamber occupancy or escape is an upstream movement endpoint and does not directly measure landing, probing, blood feeding, or biting. In arm-in-cage tracking, topical repellents reduced bites even though mosquitoes made multiple brief skin contacts, showing that encounter, contact, and bite can diverge. A human-protection claim therefore needs a direct human-contact endpoint rather than occupancy alone.

Record immediate knockdown at the end of exposure, repeated recovery or normal-movement status after removal, and delayed mortality such as a 24-hour endpoint. Follow the same exposed cohort and report dead, knocked down, recovered, and behaviorally normal categories against a matched control. A motionless mosquito at exposure end is not necessarily dead, and recovery is not the same endpoint as survival or voluntary avoidance during exposure.

Table 8 labels N=6 and reports a mean Aedes aegypti protection time of 4.0 +/- 0.0 hours for F3, a 10 percent total citronella formulation containing a 1:1 mixture of free oil and citronella-loaded nanostructured lipid carrier. F1, 10 percent free citronella oil in the same oil-in-water emulsion base, averaged 0.3 +/- 0.5 hours. However, Methods says that four formulations were evaluated on three participants, so the human sample size is unresolved and the table label must not be silently converted into six distinct volunteers. The study measured skin permeation and thermal properties, but it did not directly measure volatile release rate. The authors inferred prolonged release from the formulation, thermal behavior, permeation and mass-balance evidence, and efficacy. It does not show that every controlled-release citronella formulation protects for four hours, nor does it establish durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, varied climates, natural use, or prevention of mosquito-borne disease.

The evidence is mixed and endpoint-specific. The 2016 receptor-family paper annotated and compared SWD odorant and gustatory receptors, which identifies candidates but does not establish that one receptor causes avoidance. The 2015 avoidance study measured SWD behavior to several odorants, but it did not perturb a specific SWD receptor to prove necessity. The 2026 PNAS study did perform receptor perturbations and found two receptors required for attraction to ripe fruit, while those receptors were dispensable for egg-laying preference; that is causal attraction evidence, not causal repellent avoidance. A specific receptor can be said to drive SWD avoidance only when a selective perturbation changes avoidance to the same compound in the same behavioral assay, with locomotion and toxicity controlled. None of these sources establishes that full chain for a crop-repellent candidate.

Repeat the comparison: visually match treatment and control, or hide the source hardware while randomizing positions. Drosophila suzukii showed color preferences, and the preferred visual cue changed with the accompanying odor in the cited study. The source measured trap-oriented responses, not oviposition, so it does not prove an egg-laying effect. Vision and olfaction should be treated as interacting modalities rather than independent additive cues.

Compare naive and pre-exposed flies at defined exposure and washout times, then repeat the same avoidance, activity, survival, and oviposition endpoints after removing the volatile. The cited study found no loss of deterrence to octenol or 2-pentylfuran after 24, 48, or 72 hours of adult pre-exposure or after larval exposure, but it did not find a geosmin repellent effect in its assay. That compound-specific result does not prove long-term field persistence after removal or weathering.

The cited SWD study used 5- to 10-day-old adults and compared fed flies with flies deprived of food for 15 hours; starvation-induced locomotion increased activity. It also found lower food seeking in virgin females than in mated gravid females and linked reduced female foraging with lower oviposition in microbiome-deprived flies. These direct results show that hunger, mating state, gravidity, and microbiome can change the behavioral baseline against which a repellent is judged. Age could change reproductive maturity, and prior egg laying could change remaining egg load and oviposition motivation, but this paper did not experimentally measure either effect. Standardize, block, or deliberately vary age, mating, feeding, microbiome, and prior egg-laying history, and do not infer unmeasured age or prior-oviposition effect sizes from the mating-state result.

A crop canopy adds airflow, source spacing and placement, foliage, temperature, timing, and changing release-rate effects that a still-air chamber does not estimate. In 2016 raspberry plots, automated 1-octen-3-ol puffers reduced SWD oviposition by 20% versus controls. In 2017, puffers were 42-55% more effective than passive vials, and dawn and dusk release performed similarly to continuous daytime release. No placement difference was detected, but low fly abundance limited that comparison, so larger-scale perimeter and placement trials remain necessary. These results are specific to the tested crop, compound, years, pressure, and delivery methods.

Distinguish directed orientation from general motor suppression with separate behavioral and locomotor controls. In the odor plume, measure heading and upwind or downwind movement, turn and exit angle, time inside the plume or treated zone, crosswind displacement, re-entry, and source approach or contact. In matched clean air without the odor plume, measure flight speed, total distance, immobility, takeoff or flight propensity, knockdown, survival, and recovery after exposure. A selective change in trajectory or plume occupancy with preserved locomotion supports an orientation effect; slowing in both plume and clean-air settings supports impairment instead. Randomize side and order, blind tracking, match airflow, solvent, and release rate, and standardize age, sex, mating, feeding, and circadian state. The cited wind-tunnel study directly measured takeoff, upwind flight, and source contact in virgin, starved female SWD, while the locomotor study showed that activity varies strongly with mating state and daylight. Together they support the endpoint design, but they do not establish that any candidate volatile is a repellent.

No. The 283.03 m result is the maximum distance recorded from one Drosophila suzukii that flew on a tethered flight mill during an approximately 24-hour laboratory assay; it is not a measured field dispersal distance, plume reach, or crop-protection threshold. Across both apparatuses, the study tested 401 adults and only 15.7% initiated flight: 18.0% in the free-flight chamber and 13.4% on the tethered mill. Apparatus changed behavior. Summer morphs were reported as 14.0% more likely to initiate flight in the chamber than on the mill, and mean first-flight duration was 36.7 seconds in free flight versus 11.7 seconds on the mill. The defensible inference is that tethering and assay geometry materially affect estimated flight propensity and duration, so a single mill maximum cannot define even a minimum field buffer. Set buffer width from direct field evidence that measures movement or mark-release-recapture, plume concentration and persistence across the crop edge, immigration, and fruit infestation or damage at candidate distances. Until those measurements exist, 283.03 m is a laboratory capacity outlier, not a design distance.

At the intended formulation and dose, measure crop phytotoxicity and quality, residues and worker exposure, pollinator and beneficial-arthropod survival and behavior, and relevant soil or aquatic exposure. The cited feeding study compared SWD with two pollinator bee species, but greater tolerance in those bees does not establish field safety for a different repellent, formulation, route, crop, or dose. That study also does not measure crop injury, field exposure, predators, parasitoids, soil organisms, aquatic organisms, or worker exposure, so each remains a separate test requirement rather than an inferred safety result.

Habituation is supported by a within-individual or within-generation decline after nonreinforced exposure that recovers after sufficient washout. Inherited resistance requires a persistent phenotype in offspring raised without exposure and ideally a selection response or genetic association. The cited SWD study found no habituation to octenol or 2-pentylfuran under its adult and larval pre-exposure conditions, but it did not test genetic association, rear unexposed offspring, select lines across generations, measure cross-resistance, or estimate field phenotype frequencies. Use common-garden offspring and unexposed-generation designs to separate learning, parental effects, and inheritance.

Measure eggs per fruit, hatch or larval establishment, pupation or adult emergence, fruit damage and decay, rejected fruit, marketable yield, and economic loss using consistent fruit denominators. Record cultivar, fruit condition, and ripeness as separate factors. The push-pull field work measured fewer pupae emerging from treated raspberries. In the cherry study, the developmental experiment used overripe fruit and found cultivar differences in pupation (0.80-0.87) and survival (0.51-0.62); it did not compare developmental outcomes across ripeness states. That study's ripeness results concern fruit traits and oviposition, so they do not establish a ripeness effect on later development or survival. Every prevented egg would not necessarily have become a damaging larva. Neither cited study measured the complete chain through marketable yield, economic loss, or operational fit, so those outcomes must be measured directly rather than inferred from pupal emergence.

Aedes aegypti host seeking is multimodal, but the cues are not fully interchangeable. The cited primary studies directly support integration of carbon dioxide, human odor, and thermal infrared; they also show that removing two visual rhodopsins abolished vision-guided target attraction while leaving odor tracking normal, and that carbon dioxide and a blood meal changed visual tracking. The thermal-infrared paper identifies close-range moisture and convective body heat as contributing when mosquitoes are very near skin. It distinguishes convective heat, which is sensed at less than 10 cm, from thermal infrared, which can provide a mid-range directional cue; neither is interchangeable with carbon dioxide, odor, or vision. The cited experiments do not directly manipulate humidity or moisture within this multimodal cue combination. That is a source gap, so the degree to which humidity is redundant with odor, vision, or heat is not established here. Cue weighting depends on physiological state and assay geometry; distinguish activation, long-range orientation, landing, and biting.

Aedes aegypti has daily host-seeking patterns, and disabling the cycle clock gene changed locomotor rhythms, host-odor response, and blood-feeding propensity. Run repellent trials at a fixed time after lights-on or balance treatments across time blocks while holding light-dark entrainment, age, feeding state, and temperature consistent. Separate endogenous circadian state from immediate light, temperature, and feeding effects; a cycle knockout does not supply the exact normal hourly effect size for every wild population.

Yes, under specific laboratory conditions. In one direct repellent study, previous DEET exposure was followed by reduced behavioral repellency when Aedes aegypti were tested three hours later, together with a reduced electroantennogram response to DEET. A separate associative learning study trained females with 10 pairings of a 60-second odor presentation and mechanical shock separated by 2-minute intervals, then found learned avoidance of mammalian-associated odorants 24 hours later, but not the same change for the floral odor linalool. The DEET result is short-term pre-exposure evidence, while the shock experiment is associative learning. The DEET result does not establish long-term persistence or field product failure, and the learning experiment does not show learning to every repellent. Habituation, sensitization, learned association, innate repellency, and toxic impairment remain separate explanations.

Population background can change a result enough to reverse the practical interpretation. At the same 0.00852% initial transfluthrin loading on paper, non-contact escape was 5.0% in the laboratory USDA strain and 54.9% in the field NON population. That is one assay and one population comparison; the loading is the initial paper loading, not a measured airborne concentration at the mosquito. A separate resistance-selection study found changes in longevity, blood-feeding avidity, egg output, and development, but it does not quantify repellent response by age, genotype, or resistance state. Report and test population, genotype or resistance phenotype, age, mating, and feeding state across relevant backgrounds. Do not assume one colony represents the species or that insecticide resistance changes repellency in one fixed direction.

Two full-field experiments found negligible reductions in human landing by wild Aedes aegypti, whereas large-cage semi-field tests found about 50-60% landing reduction across the tested doses, collection methods, placements, and mosquito sources. The authors judged moderate pyrethroid resistance unlikely to explain the field shortfall. The endpoint was landing, not bites, infection, or disease, and potential inhalation exposure was a concern at the highest 15 g loading. For translation, give the open-field result precedence and repeat matched field landing and biting tests with measured vapor exposure, weather, airflow, placement, population background, and safety monitoring.

In the close-proximity odor assay, Aedes aegypti and Culex quinquefasciatus were repelled to varying degrees by lemongrass oil, PMD, eugenol, and DEET, while Anopheles coluzzii responded to lemongrass oil and PMD but not eugenol or DEET. Each test used one resting female, filter paper about 0.5 cm away, and a 30 seconds observation window. This is direct odor-only, close-range movement evidence, not human-odor masking, landing, biting, or protection time. Do not transfer an Anopheles result to Aedes or infer a universal receptor; test the intended Aedes population at the intended vapor exposure, then confirm host-seeking and protection endpoints.

Aedes aegypti did not show one universal host preference across the 27 sub-Saharan African collection sites: laboratory odor-choice behavior varied widely among populations. In the final ecological model, dry-season intensity and regional human population density explained 83% of the geographic variation, and human preference was associated with shared human-specialist ancestry. Those predictors are associations, not proof that either factor alone causes preference. Repellent development should include multiple relevant populations and keep laboratory human-versus-animal odor choice separate from field blood meals, landing, and biting.

Female Aedes aegypti showed stable, large differences in attraction to odor collected on nylon sleeves worn by the same people repeatedly over months to years. More-attractive donors tended to have higher abundances of several skin-derived carboxylic acids, and a separate validation cohort reproduced the overall association while retaining individual exceptions. Mosquitoes lacking ionotropic-receptor co-receptors showed lower overall attraction to human odor but still distinguished relatively high- from low-attractiveness donors, so those receptors and acids do not by themselves explain the whole preference. Treat donor identity and sampling day as assay variables: repeat collections, balance donor order, include multiple donors, and keep odor-sample choice separate from landing, biting, and protection endpoints. The acid result is an association, not proof that one acid causes the complete phenotype or that changing it will create an effective repellent.

Female Aedes aegypti showed the strongest preference for a roughly 34 C infrared source when carbon dioxide (CO2) and human odor were also present. Infrared, CO2, or human odor alone produced little host-seeking in this assay. TRPA1 at the antennal tip was required for infrared detection, and two co-expressed opsins supported responses at lower radiant intensities. The study separates radiant infrared from conductive or convective heat, and it measured female host-seeking rather than biting or human protection. Infrared is therefore a plausible component of host location, not a validated stand-alone repellent target or proof that blocking it alone protects people.

The transfluthrin paper reported initial loading on treated papers but says the active ingredient remaining on those papers at test time was not chemically determined. Separately, its methods do not report chamber-air concentration or plume exposure. The citronella paper measured protection time, thermal properties, and skin permeation, but it did not directly measure volatile release rate or chamber air concentration. Those findings support keeping source loading, retained source mass, and measured mosquito exposure separate, but neither paper supplies a universal reporting standard. As an R&D reporting recommendation, record active ingredient and formulation, applied mass and area, source age, retained source mass when measured, evaporation or release rate over time when measured, chamber volume and ventilation, air-sampling method and airborne concentration when measured, source-to-mosquito distance, time, temperature, humidity, and airflow. Keep loading, retained mass, emission, transport, measured concentration, distance, and behavior separate; do not convert applied mass to milligrams per cubic meter without validated release and transport measurements. The cited public evidence does not yet provide one complete product-specific package spanning carrier, concentration, application method, spatial delivery, and measured release rate; this is a source gap, not evidence that those properties are absent.

Official EPA guidance for human-skin repellent trials says to wash the test limbs with unscented detergent, rinse, and dry them; avoid alcohol, tobacco, and scented products for at least twelve hours before and throughout the test; avoid activities that increase perspiration; and avoid abrading, rubbing, touching, or wetting the treated area. It also says to prevent cross-contamination and apply a consistent standard dose with uniform coverage. These are participant-preparation and treatment-integrity controls, not proof that all person-to-person differences in mosquito attraction have been removed. Tracking diet beyond alcohol, sleep, medication, menstrual or pregnancy status, baseline attractiveness, or skin microbiome may be useful R&D design choices when scientifically and ethically justified, but this cited section does not require them or establish how to stratify them. Pre-specify any such covariates and report them as study-design decisions rather than EPA requirements.

A landing-only arm-in-cage endpoint can establish reduced landings under the stated dose, mosquito density, exposure interval, participant, and cage conditions. It does not establish fewer probes or blood meals unless those outcomes are separately measured, and it does not by itself establish distance repellency, field performance, disease prevention, or durability through sweat, washing, abrasion, and weather. EPA guidance requires the study to define and score the chosen endpoint consistently. In tracking experiments, four topical repellents reduced bites while mosquitoes still made brief skin contact, so no bite does not mean no approach or contact. Preserve landing, contact, probing, biting, and blood feeding as separate endpoints.

WHO and EPA guidance provide standardized baseline efficacy and complete-protection-time methods for human-skin repellents. EPA's baseline subject controls explicitly avoid perspiration, abrasion, rubbing, touching, and wetting the treated area, so a baseline test does not establish protection after those stresses. The wash-in and wash-off review identifies water, sweat, temperature, and abrasion as durability and reapplication factors. These sources do not provide a validated sunlight or ultraviolet challenge protocol or a universal multi-stress matrix; that is a source gap. For product R&D, a challenge design could use separate, pre-specified sweat or water, washing and drying, rubbing or abrasion, exploratory sunlight or ultraviolet, and relevant temperature arms, each with an unchallenged time-matched control. That is an R&D challenge design, not a source-standardized protocol. Repeat landing or bite protection after each challenge; retained active ingredient or measured release is supporting evidence and cannot substitute for behavioral protection.

First separate three levels: ordinary avoidance is the intended behavioral response; reduced sensitivity is a measured behavioral phenotype; and physiological resistance requires evidence of an altered physiological mechanism. As an operational decision rule, require repeatable reduced sensitivity at a standardized dose or air concentration plus inheritance evidence before calling the phenotype inherited resistance; this is not a universal definition. Heritable behavioral insensitivity does not by itself prove an altered physiological mechanism because behavioral sensitivity can also be inherited. In the direct transfluthrin study, selective mating over nine generations produced an insensitive SRA- line, the decline was significant by generation F4, and an experimental cross between SRA- females and wild-type males restored spatial-repellent sensitivity and insecticide susceptibility. The association with reduced insecticide susceptibility supports a link, but altered uptake, metabolism, target-site action, or neural processing would need direct measurement before naming a physiological mechanism. A separate clothing study also shows why kdr or insecticide-resistance status cannot automatically be equated with repellent failure. Insecticide resistance and repellent response cannot be assumed equivalent across compounds, formulations, routes, populations, or doses.

The evidence supports antennal detection and adult field attraction, not crop protection. The cited 2025 Plutella xylostella study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses and found a three-component blend that attracted adults in field traps. It did not show that the blend reduced egg laying, larval establishment, feeding damage, or yield loss. A separate direct host-choice study measured orientation, landing, fecundity, and oviposition as distinct outcomes, which is why antennal response and trap catch cannot substitute for reproductive or crop endpoints. Test the blend in randomized choice and no-choice oviposition assays and then in matched field plots that separately count adults, eggs, larvae, leaf damage, and marketable yield. Until those downstream outcomes improve, the blend is a candidate attractant rather than a demonstrated crop repellent or crop-protection treatment.

Direct Plutella xylostella evidence separates host finding from egg laying. Flying females use volatile plant cues to locate and land on hosts, while plant position and host-versus-nonhost identity also affect alighting. Direct oviposition tests found the isothiocyanates iberin and sulforaphane to be the most active volatile stimulants and also found activity in non-volatile plant fractions. Surface-choice experiments found more eggs on reduced-wax or herbicide-treated plants and on glossy rather than waxy strains, implicating epicuticular wax, although visual, chemical, and tactile mechanisms were not isolated. A separate study found that larva-induced host-plant volatiles attracted adult females in a Y-tube olfactory assay; it did not measure landing or egg deposition, so it cannot show that the attraction caused oviposition. Another study identified eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses: benzaldehyde, limonene, phenylacetaldehyde, acetophenone, linalool, 2-phenylethyl alcohol, methyl salicylate, and methyl 2-methoxybenzoate; field trapping supported a three-component blend. Antennal response, field trapping, attraction, landing, and egg deposition are not interchangeable endpoints, and antennal activity does not prove that a compound guides egg laying. Keep odor, vision, surface contact, landing, and oviposition separate when selecting or rejecting a cue.

Start with an assay ladder that separately measures adult orientation or landing, female oviposition, egg hatch, larval establishment and feeding, plant damage, and marketable yield or quality. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already demonstrate why the layers cannot be collapsed: one measured host orientation, landing, and egg laying; another separately counted adults, larvae, and leaf damage in broccoli; and the semiochemical study linked a four-choice adult assay to field population and beneficial-insect counts. Those sources do not show that one early endpoint universally predicts marketable yield. The proposed order is a product-development design, and adult avoidance alone cannot establish reduced crop damage.

SWD and mosquito work can transfer assay principles and hypotheses: separate contact from non-contact exposure, measure dose and plume, use matched controls, and follow behavior with reproduction and damage endpoints. It cannot transfer efficacy. Direct Plutella xylostella studies already provide adult choice, host-location, oviposition, field population, larval-density, and leaf-damage endpoints, so a DBM program should build on those rather than claim the species is unstudied. The candidate repellent must still be tested directly in Plutella for non-contact orientation, contact effects, sex and life-stage response, oviposition, larval feeding and damage, dose response, toxicity, field plume behavior, and persistence. Keep the SWD and Aedes papers labeled as method analogies and the DBM papers labeled as focal-species evidence.

A return to normal activity at 30 minutes rules out neither reversible knockdown nor delayed mortality, and fewer landings alone does not prove spatial avoidance. Use matched non-contact and contact arms with carrier-only controls and a measured dose or release rate. In the non-contact arm, measure approach, landing, zone occupancy, exit, flight or walking speed, total distance, and immobility over time. During and after exposure, record knockdown separately, then follow the same moths for recovery at pre-specified intervals and delayed mortality such as 24 hours. A selective reduction in approach or landing with preserved clean-air locomotion, no knockdown, and no excess mortality supports temporary spatial avoidance; general slowing, immobility, knockdown, or death supports toxic impairment. Direct Plutella xylostella host-finding work separately measured flying and alighting, while the citronella study kept movement, feeding, oviposition, mortality, and field abundance as distinct endpoints. Neither source tested this exact candidate or 10-to-30-minute recovery design, so the proposed measurement set is an R&D recommendation rather than evidence that the compound is an effective repellent.

In the ROTH Plutella xylostella strain, period and timeless transcripts showed rhythms under entrained and free-running conditions, but measured adult locomotor activity was temperature-driven and light-suppressed rather than coupled to those clock-gene rhythms. A release schedule should therefore be tested against actual adult activity under the intended light and temperature pattern, not selected from transcript peaks alone. This laboratory strain does not establish field timing across populations, seasons, canopy conditions, or weather, so molecular and locomotor endpoints must remain separate and field timing needs direct validation.

The study reported several distinct citronella endpoints in Plutella xylostella: larval movement in a filter-paper assay, feeding-related effects on treated cabbage, oviposition inhibition, mortality, and season-long field population counts. In the filter-paper assay, 3 and 4 mL/L produced 100% reported repellency at 48 hours; mortality was measured separately through 120 hours, so neither value should be treated as pure spatial repellency. Lower field abundance also cannot be assigned to repellency alone. Separate no-contact choice, treated-leaf feeding, survival or knockdown, adult oviposition, and crop damage. The reported Diadegma eucerophaga field observation is study- and species-specific, not general natural-enemy safety proof.

The missing public baseline is not whether Plutella xylostella can respond to candidate repellents. Direct studies already report airflow or Y-tube avoidance, oviposition deterrence, larval toxicity or feeding effects, field trapping, and field population outcomes for Mikania micrantha oil, Angelica pubescens extract and compounds, non-host essential oils, and citronella. Those studies use source dose or airflow, but they do not provide one field-validated chain that measures airborne concentration at the moth, separates non-contact avoidance from contact-permitted exposure and toxic impairment, and then links that same exposure to eggs, larvae, crop damage, and marketable yield. That quantified exposure-to-crop-protection chain is the highest-leverage public-evidence gap. Close it with a randomized, blinded, staged study of one focal candidate and a known DBM-active positive control. First, use mated females and standardized Brassica plants in an airflow chamber with solvent and airflow controls, an upstream source physically isolated from moth and plant, and air sampling at the moth position over time to measure plume concentration. Record orientation, landing, zone occupancy, flight, knockdown, recovery, mortality, and eggs separately across a dose series. Compare that odor-only condition with a contact-permitted treated-plant condition while measuring both headspace and surface residue; do not label a volatile-treated surface contact-only because it still emits vapor. Then carry prespecified effective, non-toxic exposures into replicated semi-field or field plots and measure plume exposure, eggs, larval establishment, feeding damage, marketable yield or quality, persistence, weather, and non-target effects. Release rate alone is not airborne concentration, and a laboratory avoidance result alone would not close the field crop-protection gap. This prioritization and design are R&D recommendations, not evidence that one candidate already works.
